column1: What separates the frontal from parietal lobe?
What separates the frontal from temporal lobe?
Broca area is in which gyrus and lobe?
Wernicke area is in which gyrus and lobe?
What are the components of the basal ganglia?
column2: Caudate nucleus, Putamen, Globus pallidus (internus/externus), Subthalamic nucleus, Substantia nigra (pars compacta + pars reticulata)

column1: Which layer of neocortex receives thalamic input?
Which layer contains Betz cells (corticofugal output to spinal cord)?
What is the primary function of the caudate nucleus?
column2: Motor planning and procedural memory; degeneration causes Huntington disease

column1: STN lesion causes what movement disorder?
column2: Hemiballismus — violent, flinging movements of contralateral limbs

column1: What are the 6 layers of the neocortex (superficial to deep)?
column2: I Molecular, II External granular, III External pyramidal, IV Internal granular, V Internal pyramidal, VI Multiform

column1: What percentage of cortex is hidden within sulci?
column2: Two-thirds (2/3) is within sulci; one-third exposed on gyri

column1: What is the blood supply of Broca and Wernicke areas?
column2: Usually LEFT hemisphere: MCA (middle cerebral artery) territory

column1: Gerstmann syndrome localizes to which gyrus?
column2: Angular gyrus of the dominant hemisphere (usually left) — Brodmann 39

column1: What separates the parietal from occipital lobe?
What is the precentral gyrus function?
column2: Primary motor cortex (M1) — Brodmann area 4 — voluntary motor control

column1: What is the postcentral gyrus function?
column2: Primary somatosensory cortex (S1) — Brodmann areas 3, 1, 2 — sensory input from body
Facilitates movement — cortex excites striatum → inhibits GPi → disinhibits thalamus → excites cortex
Inhibits movement — cortex excites striatum → activates GPi → inhibits thalamus → inhibits cortex
